english: towards the house; rgyál-poi (rgyal po'i) gán-nas (gan nas) pýin (phyin) he came from the king; in col. language also c. accus.: *ḍóg-po gán-du* (*dog po gan du*) W. close by the brook, and c. termin. case, *čur gán-te* (*chur gan te*) W. hard by the water: rir-gán-pa (rir gan pa) one living close to a mountain or hill.

and rkyal (rkyal), supine, lying on the back, with the face upward, gan-kyál (du) (gan rkyal (du)) nyál-ba (nyal ba) to lie in that position; gyel-ba (gyel ba) to fall backward; sgyél-ba (sgyel ba) to make one fall on his back; *gḥaṇ-kyál lóg-pa* (*ghan rkyal log pa*) to perform a somerset, to tumble over head and heels C.
C., *gam-rgya* (*gam rgya*) W., a written contract, an agreement.
Sch.: a silk handkerchief offered as a present in exchanging compliments on meeting, = k̄a-btags (kha btags).
store-room, storehouse Sch.

n. of a famous temple in rdo-rje-gdan (rdo rje gdan) (Vajrāsana near Gaya in Bengal) Tar. 16, 4 and elsewhere frq.; yet the words in Glr. 8, 10: pýi (phyi) gándho-la (gandho la) nán-du (nang du) lhá-k̄aṅ (lha khang) byás-pas (byas pas) 'making outwardly a gandhola, inside an idolshrine', seem not to admit of a noun proper; a Lama explained it by gtsúg-lag-k̄aṅ (gtsug lag khang); more correctly perh. = dri-gtsaṅ-k̄aṅ (dri gtsang khang), i.e. = गन्धकुट. Cf. also gḥáṇḍhola (ghandho la).
Glr. 65, 8 obviously a Ssk. word, though not in our dictionaries; Lamas described it as an architectural ornament, consisting in small turrets or spires along the edge of a flat roof.
W. a belch (vulgar).
to hide, to conceal one's self Dzl. and elsewhere frq.; gáb-yig (gab yig), writing in secret characters, cryptography W., C.; gáb-sa (gab sa) a place of concealment, hiding-place.
Glr., panels or little boards beneath the cornice of a roof, often filled out with paintings.
a tableau containing numerous mythological and astrological figures, and used for fortune-telling.

wylie: gab tshad, gab pa'i tsha ba
english: a disease Med.; acc. to Schr. a hectic, consumptive fever.
v. gan (gan).
W. citron, lemon.
1. a chest, box Pth.; a little box or case; when containing amulets, it is worn suspended by a string round the neck (v. Schl. 174). - 2. a squeaking sound W., *gau zér-ċe* (*ga'u zer ce*) to squeak.

I. (Cs. gár-ma (gar ma)) a dance, gar byed-pa (gar byed pa), W. *gár se-ċe* (*gar se ce*), to dance; glu gar rtséd-mo byéd-pa (glu gar rtsed mo byed pa) Glr. to sing, to dance and play; gár-mk̄an (gar mkhan) 1. one dancing, a dancer, a performer, e.g. even Buddha or any saint, when displaying miracles. - 2. n. of a god Tar. 11, 17, acc. to Schr., Siwa; gar-stábs (gar stabs) a dancing gesture or motion. - II. = gá-ru (ga ru), gáṅ-du (gang du), whither, whereto, where; gar yaṅ (gar yang) anywhere, gár yaṅ skyé-ba (gar yang skye ba) growing everywhere Wdn.; gar yaṅ mi gró-ba (gar yang mi gro ba) to go nowhere, to remain where one is Mil.; Pth. - *gar-méd* (*gar med*) W. at all events, by all means, = *ga-na-méd* (*ga na med*) - gar-báb (gar bab) at random, hit or miss, at hap-hazard Sch.
n. of a medicine Lt.
strong, gar-čaṅ (gar chang) strong beer.
solid, not hollow Sch.
thick, e.g. soup, = ská-ba (ska ba); gar-slá (gar sla) Sch.: thick and thin; thickness.

native name of the district called by the Hindoos Lāhul or Lāhōl (acc to Cunningham 'Lahul' is a corruption of lho-yul (lho yul), southern country, which latter appellation, however, is not in use in that district itself). Here, in the village of Kyelang, a missionary station was established in 1857, by the Church of the United Brethren (Moravians), together with a school and a lithographic press, for dif-
